planting.note: Plant plugs or container divisions in spring in very well-drained, sandy, dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sea oats is the primary dune-stabilizing grass of the Gulf Coast and Atlantic shore and plays a critical ecological role in dune formation and erosion control — it is protected from collection in Florida, Georgia, and several other states. Purchase only nursery-grown plants.

directions: Plant nursery-grown plugs in spring in very well-drained, dry, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun — sea oats is the signature grass of the Gulf Coast and Atlantic barrier islands and beaches, its dramatic, gracefully drooping, flat seed heads swaying in the ocean breeze on tall, upright stems from summer through winter in one of the most iconic images of the southern coast. The long, creeping rhizomes spread underground to stabilize shifting dune sand, building and maintaining the dune systems that protect coastal communities from storm surge and erosion. Sea oats is legally protected in Florida, Georgia, and other states — collect no plants from the wild and purchase only nursery-propagated plants and seed. In coastal garden settings it is outstanding for low-maintenance, drought-tolerant, ornamental dune gardens, beach property landscaping, and sandy, salt-spray-exposed areas where few other plants succeed. The seeds are an important food source for birds in the coastal community.

planting.note: Plant offsets or container specimens in spring in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; handle with extreme caution — the leaf tips are rigid, needle-sharp, and can cause serious eye and skin injury. Spanish bayonet is one of the most salt-tolerant yuccas, thriving in coastal conditions.

directions: Plant offsets or container specimens in very well-drained, dry, sandy to loamy soil in full sun — Spanish bayonet is the most commonly encountered native yucca of the Gulf Coast and Southeast Atlantic coast, forming dramatic, architectural rosettes of rigid, dark green leaves tipped with needle-sharp points (hence 'bayonet') on stems that can grow 6–10 feet tall and develop a trunk-like stem over years. Mature plants are truly striking landscape specimens. Enormous panicles of white, pendulous, edible flowers appear in spring-summer; the large, fleshy fruit capsules and flowers are both edible and were important food sources for Native Americans. Plant well away from foot traffic areas — the razor-sharp leaf tips are a serious safety hazard. Extraordinary drought, salt spray, and wind tolerance makes it one of the best structural plants for coastal, dune, and xeriscape landscapes. Deer-proof, essentially pest-free, and immortal in adapted sandy soils.

planting.note: Plant offsets or container specimens in spring in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Gulf Coast yucca grows naturally in the sandy coastal prairies and open pine flatwoods of eastern Texas and Louisiana and is extremely well adapted to the heat, humidity, and poor soils of the Gulf Coast.

directions: Plant offsets or container specimens in very well-drained, dry, sandy to loamy soil in full sun — Gulf Coast yucca is a stemless or short-stemmed native yucca endemic to the sandy coastal prairies and pine flatwoods of Louisiana and East Texas, forming low rosettes of long, flexible, dark blue-green leaves with softer, less-rigid tips than Spanish bayonet (Yucca aloifolia). Tall, upright flower stalks 3–6 feet emerge in early summer bearing large clusters of creamy-white, bell-shaped flowers that are the exclusive food source for the yucca moth (Tegeticula sp.), which simultaneously pollinates the flowers and lays its eggs in the ovary — one of the most specialized and obligate plant-pollinator relationships in nature. The relationship is so specific that Gulf Coast yucca will not set seed without its yucca moth pollinator. An excellent, low-maintenance native structural plant for sandy, poor soils, drought garden designs, and coastal prairie restoration.

planting.note: Plant offsets (pups) in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; aloe vera tolerates light frost to about 28°F but is killed by prolonged hard freezes — in zone 8 and colder, grow in containers that can be moved indoors or provide heavy mulch and frost protection.

directions: Plant offsets in very well-drained, dry, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade — aloe vera is one of the oldest cultivated plants in human history, grown for millennia in tropical and subtropical climates as the premier first-aid plant for minor burns, sunburns, cuts, insect bites, and skin irritation; the clear gel inside the thick, fleshy leaves contains anti-inflammatory and wound-healing compounds including polysaccharides, vitamins, and amino acids. Break off a lower leaf and apply the clear gel directly to burns for immediate cooling relief. In zone 9–12 it grows as an outdoor landscape succulent, tolerating drought, poor soil, reflected heat, and neglect with remarkable resilience; it produces dramatic yellow to orange tubular flower spikes in late winter on established plants. It spreads prolifically by offsets (pups) that form around the base — remove and replant pups to create new plants. Every household and kitchen garden should have one within reach.
sources: National Institutes of Health — National Library of Medicine

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moist to moderately dry, loamy to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie gentian grows naturally in wet prairies, roadsides, and disturbed areas in the Gulf Coast region and self-sows readily in open, sunny, slightly alkaline conditions.

directions: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moderately moist to dry soil in full sun — prairie gentian is the wild ancestor of the popular florist lisianthus, a Gulf Coast native annual of wet prairies and roadsides from Texas to Florida that produces elegant, upward-facing, funnel-shaped flowers in clear blue-purple with a velvety texture and a prominent yellow and white center — one of the most beautiful wildflower blooms on the Gulf Coast prairies. The florist lisianthus (E. grandiflorum) is a cultivated form of this species bred for larger, showier, often double flowers in expanded color ranges. The native species is shorter, tougher, and self-sows freely in Gulf Coast gardens, returning annually in open, sunny, slightly alkaline areas. The silvery-blue to blue-gray, glaucous, clasping leaves are attractive even out of bloom. A lovely wildflower for naturalistic prairie plantings, roadsides, and open, sunny garden borders in the Gulf Coast region.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, loamy to rocky, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; ground plum has a deep taproot that makes it drought tolerant but very difficult to transplant — sow directly in its permanent location.

directions: Direct sow scarified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — ground plum is a native sprawling legume of the southern Great Plains and central prairies, historically one of the most important native edible plants of the southern prairie, producing clusters of purple to reddish-purple pea flowers in April–May followed by the remarkable, plum-like fruit pods — green when young, reddish when ripe — that gave the plant its common name. The young, unripe pods have a mild, slightly sweet, pea-like flavor and were eaten raw by Plains peoples as a seasonal vegetable and green snack food on the prairie. The pods are edible raw when fully green and firm, but become papery when ripe. An important nitrogen-fixing prairie legume that improves soil for neighboring plants. Outstanding for prairie restoration plantings in well-drained, alkaline, or rocky soils in zone 3–9.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist, loamy to rocky soil in full sun; cottage pinks require excellent drainage and will not survive in wet or heavy clay soils. Avoid overhead watering to prevent crown rot.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — cottage pinks are the classic old-fashioned garden pinks of European cottage gardens, producing intensely fragrant, fringed (hence 'plumarius' — feathery), five-petaled flowers in shades of white, pink, rose, crimson, and bicolors in May–June on a low, spreading mat of glaucous blue-green, grass-like foliage that remains attractive year-round. The rich, spicy-sweet, clove-like fragrance is extraordinary — unmatched by modern hybrid dianthus — and one of the defining scents of the traditional cottage garden. They require excellent drainage and perform best in slightly alkaline soils, making them excellent choices for rock gardens, dry stone walls, gravel gardens, and raised beds with sharply drained soil. Deadhead spent flowers to extend bloom; shear plants by one-third after flowering to maintain a tidy, compact mound.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky to sandy soil in full sun; cheddar pinks are among the most drought and heat tolerant of all Dianthus species — they are exceptional in alkaline, rocky, or sandy soils where other perennials fail.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky to sandy soil in full sun — cheddar pinks are native to the limestone gorges of Cheddar, England and are among the most drought-tolerant and heat-resistant perennial dianthus available, forming compact, mat-like mounds of very narrow, blue-gray, evergreen foliage tipped with intensely fragrant, deep rose-pink to cerise flowers in late spring. The rich clove fragrance is as strong as cottage pinks but the plant is smaller, tidier, and notably more heat and alkaline-soil tolerant. The Perennial Plant Association named it Perennial Plant of the Year in 2015. Superb for rock gardens, dry walls, gravel gardens, and edging in alkaline or sandy soils where drainage is excellent. Shear lightly after bloom to maintain compact mounds and often promotes a second flush of bloom in fall. Very long-lived in appropriate conditions; clumps may persist for decades.

planting.note: Direct sow seed after last frost in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Rocky Mountain bee plant self-sows prolifically and naturalizes in open, sunny, dry areas — no supplemental water is needed once plants are established.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun — Rocky Mountain bee plant is a native annual of the Great Plains, Rocky Mountain foothills, and desert Southwest, producing clusters of showy, spidery, pink to white flowers on tall, branching, slightly pungent-scented stems from summer through fall. It is one of the most important native bee plants of the central US: bumblebees, mason bees, and honey bees are intensely attracted to the nectar-rich flowers, working the plants all day in good weather. The plant was an important food source for indigenous peoples of the Plains and Pueblos, who boiled the leaves as a green vegetable and rendered the plant to make a black dye used in pottery painting. It self-sows prolifically to naturalize in dry, open, sandy or rocky soils across a wide climate range. The musky scent (a defense against some insect herbivores) is more noticeable up close than at a distance.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; southern meadow rue requires consistently moist, humus-rich soil and partial to deep shade — it does not tolerate drought or full sun in hot climates.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — southern meadow rue is a rare Appalachian native of moist, rich, shaded mountain woodlands and stream-bank forests in Virginia, North Carolina, and Tennessee, producing the classic meadow-rue effect at an impressive scale: tall (3–6 foot) stems clothed in beautiful, blue-green, columbine-like compound leaves, topped in summer with loose, airy panicles of small white flowers with prominent stamens that create a soft, misty cloud-like effect above the foliage. The delicate, divided foliage is exceptionally ornamental throughout the season. It is one of the taller native shade perennials available, providing dramatic vertical interest in shaded borders, woodland gardens, and moist stream-bank plantings. At the northern edge of zone 8 in moist, deeply shaded, well-drained positions it can succeed, but it is most reliable in zone 4–7 woodland conditions.
